data.title: IBUY: Retail Plays Face Tariffs And Worsening Consumer Sentiment
data.description:
Main Thesis / Background

The purpose of this article is to evaluate the Amplify Online Retail ETF (NYSEARCA:IBUY) as an investment option at its current market price. The fund's objective is "to provide investment results that, before fees and expenses, correspond generally to the price performance of the EQM Online Retail Index. The Index is a globally diverse basket of publicly traded companies that obtain 70% or more of revenue from online or virtual sales".

I have been for and against IBUY at various times depending on the macro-backdrop. This is consistent with most of my sector ideas. I look for contrarian plays when diving into individual sectors, and IBUY is especially interesting given its focus and international exposure. However, it has not been a fund I have favored for a while, and most recently put a "hold" rating on it when I covered it a year and a half ago. Looking back, this call is partially vindicated. The fund has seen a gain, yes, but it has lagged the broader market:

The good news for buyers here is this fund, along with most discretionary sectors right now, has seen a sharp decline. This got me thinking - does the recent drop present a buying opportunity where I didn't see one before?

Unfortunately, I think the answer is "no" here. While I do see some positives, which I will discuss in this review, I can't get past the obvious headwinds facing the retail sector. These include newly announced tariffs, stubborn inflation (which could get worse going forward), and a decline in consumer sentiment in the United States. For me, I continue to see patience as a virtue for IBUY, and will be keeping my "hold" rating in place. I will use this article to justify this outlook.

Let's Start With The Obvious: Tariffs

As alluded to above, I am very concerned with the prospect of higher tariffs on American imports as it relates to equity prices. This is a headwind I have been discussing since late last year, and it has begun to sink into markets as I figured it would. The problem is not just the tariffs themselves, but the magnitude of them. While the US has always had some level of tariffs - and so has much of the world - the list given by President Trump has week has dramatically changed what we should expect to see for duties on imports going forward. While they vary sharply by country and item, the average effective tariff rate is set to soar to highs not seen since the beginning of the century, as illustrated in the graphic below:

What this means is at least two things - unless we see a dramatic shift by the Trump administration - which I don't believe is likely.

One, this creates a tremendous amount of uncertainty. Both businesses and consumers are going to need some time to adjust and evaluate what they want and need. This will result in a short-term headwind for discretionary spending, especially as importers and consumers consider how much (more) they are willing and able to pay for certain goods.

Two, the outcome from this is probably going to be a reduction of spending across the board. The premise is two-fold here. One, consumers are going to have to contend with higher prices and that almost always causes a reduction in spending, partly because they have to and partly because some will be in wait-and-see mode to hopefully ride out the tariffs (the premise being once the tariffs are removed or reduced these consumers will buy again).

The other factor is that tariffs, as with all taxes, reduce the money available for consumption. As with sales taxes, that money will flow to the government. While that is not necessarily a "bad" thing if the government puts the funds to work in a productive manner, it nevertheless siphons that money away from public consumption. Given that IBUY is a fund heavily (or one could say exclusively) reliant on consumer spending, it is difficult to come up with a bull case for this fund in this macro-environment.

Consumer Sentiment Is Down Sharply

The other major concern is consumer sentiment. American households are not surprisingly anxious about what the economy holds due to a variety of factors. This has been ongoing for a while now - even before the tariff announcements. But, as you can see, going into April sentiment has fallen sharply:

This isn't a good sign, for the US economy, and especially not for consumer-oriented investments. My worry here is that IBUY in particular is filled with companies that offer discretionary goods and services. For example, its top holdings are things like delivery services, travel sites and online shopping platforms:

The takeaway for me is that IBUY is not offering much exposure to the "needs" of consumers in the sense that it owns a lot of discretionary exposure rather than staples. This is important because when consumers cut back, they can only cut back on their needs so much. Further, the staples are a more defensive sub-sector within the consumer realm because they can actually benefit during tough or recessionary time periods. This is true when consumers downshift their preferences (such as going from brand names to store labels) and the like. But the discretionary sector doesn't benefit from recessions in the same manner.

The ultimate point I am making here is IBUY is more of a risk-on play, and that is not what I want to be doing at these levels. The market has started to correct and there is the potential for a contrarian move, but I don't think the time is ripe for that just yet. If consumers do pull back - and I believe they will - I don't see how IBUY is able to "beat" the market. That makes a neutral / hold rating the best I can do for now.

The Shift Has Been Away From "Risk"

Expanding on the prior paragraph, readers should recognize the market has shifted from a risk-taking mode not just in the past week but for the last few months. If we look at Q1 performance (which excludes the sharp sell-off from last Thursday and Friday (4/3 and 4/4/), we see that safety was top of mind for many already. I say this because gold was one of the top-performing assets in Q1, which is seen as a safe-haven and defensive play. Other areas in the green include US bonds, value stocks, and even cash. By contrast, riskier sectors such as US large-caps, growth stocks, and Bitcoin all saw noticeable draw-downs:

The relevance is, I believe this helps support why I am not a bull on a discretionary fund like IBUY. I mentioned that IBUY is a more risk-seeking ETF, and that is just not where the market has been rotating towards in the last few months. Could that change? Of course - and at some point it will. But I see more pain ahead due to tariffs and weak consumer sentiment, as I noted above. This is central to why I would suggest holding off on any new positions at the moment.

Any Positives? Online Rises Keep Rising

So far, this review has had a negative tone, and I think there are valid reasons for that. But I'm not a complete bear here. For one reason, markets have already been selling off. So it is a bit late to the game to be screaming "fire" and panic selling because the worst may be almost over. For another reason, online commerce continues to be a dominant force in the world of retail. As noted in the opening paragraph, IBUY is not an average retail fund. It specifically targets companies with an e-commerce / online platform, and that is certainly been a winning trend over the last few decades.

The good news is this is not a fad nor a trend that is running out of team. The climb of e-commerce's share of total retail sales (in the US) continues to be seen at a steady, consistent clip:

What I see here is a solid rationale for wanting exposure to online retail plays, and that is precisely what IBUY provides. While I disagree that now is the best time to initiate or add to positions, there is no denying this is a generation theme that investors may want an allocation to. For me, this positive attribute is a key reason why I am not getting too negative on this fund and feel its inclusion in the review helps justify why I think "hold" is appropriate as opposed to a "sell" rating.

IBUY Has Non-US Exposure

Another positive for IBUY in the current climate is this fund is not entirely reliant on the US consumer. While domestic consumer spending is often a strong catalyst for bull markets here, I'm not sure that will be the case in Q2. But the good news for investors in this fund is that this ETF offers a range of exposure across the globe. The following is a snapshot of the different countries that are home to the companies making up IBUY's portfolio:

Given that my concerns right now stem from what is going on in the US, I view IBUY's foreign exposure as an opportunity. It means this fund is less exposed to the geopolitics rattling US markets right now than its peers that are solely US-focused. While I am generally US-centric in my holdings, I believe now is a good time to diversify beyond our borders. IBUY does offer investors a way to do just that.

Bottom-Line

IBUY has been a modest performer over time and I see that pattern continuing in the months ahead. Well, the recent equity sell-off could open up an opportunity for this fund, I am under the impression that patient investors can get a better price in the future.

The ongoing tariff battle between the US and the rest of the world is likely to put a dent on consumer discretionary spending, and that is going to hurt this fund. Further, while IBUY is a global ETF, the majority (roughly 70%) of its holdings are US-based. With consumer sentiment in the US declining sharply, now isn't a great time to go long the US consumer. As a result, I am going to maintain my neutral "hold" rating on this fund and I suggest my followers approach any new positions selectively at this time.

data.title: Gold Market Commentary: When The Wells Run Dry
data.description:
Strong euro and tariff fears drive gold

Another month, another set of new highs. Gold finished March at US$3,115/oz, a gain of 9.9% m/m.1 Even a materially weaker US dollar, primarily via euro strength, couldn’t prevent a stellar performance and new highs across all other major currencies (Table 1).

According to our Gold Return Attribution Model (GRAM), euro strength and thus US dollar weakness was once again a key driver of gold’s performance, alongside an increase in geopolitical risk capturing tariff fears (Chart 1). Gold ETF buying continued apace in March, with all regions contributing. US funds led the charge with US$6bn (67t) of net inflows, followed by Europe then Asia with approximately US$1bn each. While ETF flows were positive, COMEX futures declined marginally by US$400mn (5t) likely on profit taking.

Chart 1: A materially weaker dollar and tariff fears drove gold to new highs in March

Table 1: Gold hit new highs during March

Gold price and performance in key currencies*

USD(oz) EUR(oz) JPY(g) GBP(oz) CAD(oz) CHF(oz) INR(10g) RMB(g) TRY(oz) AUD(oz)
March price* 3,115 2,881 15,013 2,413 4,478 2,755 88,691 728 118,256 4,991
March return* 9.9% 5.5% 9.4% 7.1% 9.3% 7.6% 4.6% 8.4% 14.4% 9.3%
Y-t-d return* 19.4% 14.3% 13.7% 15.8% 19.3% 16.3% 16.8% 18.2% 28.2% 18.3%
Record high price* 3,115 2,881 15,013 2,413 4,478 2,755 88,691 728 118,256 4,991
Record high date* 31-Mar2025 31-Mar2025 31-Mar2025 31-Mar2025 31-Mar2025 31-Mar2025 28-Mar2025 31-Mar2025 31-Mar2025 31-Mar2025

Source: Bloomberg, World Gold Council

*As of 31 March 2025. Based on the LBMA Gold Price PM in USD, expressed in local currencies, except for India and China where the MCX Gold Price PM and Shanghai Gold Benchmark PM are used, respectively.

When the wells run dry

  • Liquidity matters, and has arguably been bolstering both financial assets and the economy in the US for much of the post-COVID period
  • In 2022, however, US financial conditions tightened forcefully as liquidity was removed from markets. This perfect storm caused a very rare annual joint decline in bonds and equities. Gold held up but also experienced some bumps along the way
  • We are now at a similar impasse in liquidity conditions, but with crucial differences that bode well fundamentally for gold
  • The one hurdle is the hitherto strong run-up in gold prices. Comparisons to the 2011 and 2020 peaks are likely to be made, but in our view, the environment remains supportive of further gains.

US… aid

While by no means the sole contributors to their solid performance, the US economy and financial markets benefited from monetary and fiscal support since the COVID pandemic.

Fiscal spending programmes arguably bolstered job creation via government and government-adjacent jobs (Chart 2). Capital markets were also aided by fiscal liquidity provisions combined with a continuation of the ‘monetary backstop’ from the Fed. This helped compress the Treasury bond risk premium to well below its pre-COVID average and equity multiples to well above their pre-COVID averages (Chart 3).

Chart 2: Employment growth mostly in public sector

Chart 3: Fiscal boost and monetary backstop has helped assets

These sources of liquidity ebbed in 2022, coinciding with a rare joint decline in bond and equity markets (a 60/40 blend of S&P 500 and US treasuries dropped as well as economic activity). Gold also succumbed, falling 20% over two quarters in 2022, before a recovery to end the year flat. Proving direct causality is difficult, yet it does suggest markets and the economy had grown accustomed to artificial support.

At a crossroads

While much of the conversation over the past week has centered around tariffs, liquidity risk remains an important undercurrent. And we believe we may now be approaching a similar impasse to what markets experienced in 2022.

Quantitative tightening is slowing, but there has been no mention of a resumption of quantitative easing. Indeed, the appetite might not be there given the high levels of debt and sticky inflation. In addition, constraints on government spending via the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) are stifling fiscal support. And the Fed’s Overnight Reverse Repo facility (ON RRP) is low, which provides less wiggle room for the Fed to manage liquidity issues. This appears to be showing up in stats like order-book liquidity for equity futures and – as flagged in the Fed’s financial stability report in November3 – on-the-run bond liquidity. It may also be contributing to the year-to-date equity rout.

Chart 4: Liquidity punchbowl has been important for assets and the economy

And the labour market is flirting with contraction as hours worked are in steep decline. Logically, they lead to an employment slowdown as companies reduce hours for staff before layoffs; statistically, this also appears to be the case.4 But layoffs are also now on the rise and are likely to feed into payroll numbers in due course (Chart 5). To add to this, uncertainty surrounding tariffs has supercharged concerns about the resiliency of labour markets in the short and medium term.

Chart 5: Payrolls flirting with their first negative print

Similar but different

Should we see a similar drying up of liquidity, it’s likely to be different to 2022.

  • While inflation was rising more in 2022, it was driven by growth. This time around, inflation is sticky while growth is faltering, resulting in a stagflationary environment. In this context, rates are unlikely to be going up from here and further weakening of the dollar is likely on waning US exceptionalism
  • Central banks have been strong contributors to gold’s performance over the past three years and show few signs of letting up, adding fundamental support to prices
  • US gold ETF investors had built up sizeable holdings in 2020 prior to the 2022 wobbles. But they have been sidelined until recently, suggesting capacity to keep adding.

Fundamentals remain in place…

The current run-up in prices has taken many by surprise. Paraphrasing an old adage, shouldn’t high prices for a commodity cure high prices? Gold is not a commodity in the traditional sense and primary production’s response may have only limited impact on price. The willingness to hold and reluctance to sell – given current extreme policy uncertainty – could generate real momentum. By historical standards, the current rally isn’t particularly large or long. And comparing the current rally to the recent 2011 and 2020 peaks highlights that, relatively speaking, fundamentals look more solid (Table 2):

Table 2: Gold fundamentals in better shape

Gold US ETF Scorecard USD (oz) EUR (oz) JPY (g)
Metrics 2011 2020 2025
US Gold ETF % of US ETFs 7.2 2.1 1.6
US 10-year TIPs yield 0.1 -1.0 1.8
Tips yield 12m later -0.7 -1.2 –
S&P 500 forward P/E 12.2 25.7 20.9
Forward P/E 12m later 13.6 22.0 –
US HY spread to 10-year Tsy 6.3 4.8 3.5
HY to 10Y Tsy spread 12m later 5.2 2.7 –
Citi broad REER dollar 78.0 98.3 109.7
REER dollar 12m later 81.6 95.2 –

Source: Bloomberg, World Gold Council

*Data referenced to 31 August 2011, 30 June 2020 and 31 March 2025. HY=High yield. REER= Real Effective Exchange Rate.
  • US gold ETFs are a considerably smaller share of all US ETF assets than during 2011, as ETF buyers have been on the sidelines for the best part of four years they are not overbought
  • Real yields are higher and above their long-run average, suggesting more downside than upside risk for yields – and vice versa for gold prices
  • Forward equity price-to-earnings remains high, and that provides capacity for further downside to equities should an economic slowdown and earnings downgrades worsen, especially in the current geoeconomic conditions, a boon for gold’s safe-haven appeal
  • Credit spreads are considerably tighter than during the two previous peaks. Again, widening risks trump contraction risk, and are also gold supportive.
  • The dollar remains elevated relative to prior periods, even if it has weakened since the start of the year. With the Trump administration favouring a weaker dollar and the uncertain effect of tariffs, this could serve as an additional tailwind for gold.

…But not without risks

But we also caution that there are risks for the gold price after a rally such as this in such a short space of time.

Treasury managers at central banks could prudently slow their pace of buying given the price rally, as we saw with some central banks last year. While consumer demand adapts to higher prices eventually, the speed of price moves is like to dampen net buying in the near term. A liquidity-crunch could negatively affect gold as the most liquid assets are sold to meet margin calls.5 Additionally, geopolitical and policy nervousness is quite elevated, particularly given significant uncertainty about tariffs and its effect on market volatility, which is likely adding a meaningful premium to gold prices. Any resolution could bring that premium out, as we have seen in previous historical periods.

Chart 6: The current rally is impressive but has yet to surpass even 2011 and 2020

In sum...

The extent and speed of gold’s rally has drawn out comparisons to previous peaks. While there are headwinds that the gold market will naturally face in this environment, our analysis also suggests that current macroeconomic conditions are quite different to prior periods when the gold market reached previous highs.

Footnotes

1Based on the LBMA Gold Price PM as of 31 March 2025. Gold recorded an all-time high of US$2,936.85/oz on 19 February 2025.

2CEA Chair Stephen Miran: We're transiting the economy to be primarily driven by private sector.

3Financial Stability Report, November 2024.

4Engle Granger causality test shows a statistically significant lead relationship between hours worked and non-farm payrolls between lags of 1 to 4 months (p-values less than 5%) but not the other way around. Dummy used in 2020 to capture COVID anomalies.

5Hedge funds hit with the steepest margin calls since the 2020 Covid crisis

data.title: Myomo: Balancing CMS Tailwinds With Long-Term Risks
data.description:
Introduction

My last look at Myomo (NYSE:MYO) followed its Q3 2024 earnings. Myomo markets MyoPro, which is described as an "electronically controlled brace that may help a user regain function in arms and hands." Despite the original device achieving approval way back in 2007, Myomo's latest iteration generated just $9.2 million in Q3 2024.

Notably, in January 2024, the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services, or CMS, reclassified MyoPro as a “brace,” making it possible for the company to receive reimbursement on a lump sum basis. Despite this development, Myomo still reported net losses throughout 2024 and its valuation already implied lofty expectations of growth and profitability. This made me cautious and my rating on MYO was “Hold.”

The stock has since fallen by 28% compared to S&P 500 losses of 20% during the same period.

The following article revisits Myomo after its full year 2024 earnings.

Earnings

Digging into its Q4 earnings report, revenue of $12.07 million beat estimates by $2.17 million. The cost of revenue was $3.453 million. Gross profit was $8.615 million (71.4% gross margin). R&D, SC&M, and G&A expenses were $1.559 million, $3.696 million, and $3.6 million, respectively. The net loss in Q4 was $260,081 (compared to $2.46 million in Q4 2023).

Myomo noted it achieved positive cash flow from operations of $3.4 million. 1,389 MyoPro candidates were in the “patient pipeline” at the end of 2024. This represents a 33% year-over-year increase.

As for Q1, revenue expectations are between $9 million and $9.5 million, representing 54% to 63% year-over-year growth. For the full year 2025, Myomo anticipates revenues between $50 million and $53 million. The company expects to achieve consistent cash flow positivity by Q4 2025.

Financial Health

Looking at the balance sheet, as of December 31, cash and cash equivalents were $24.372 million. Total current assets were $32.79 million, while total current liabilities were just $10.17 million. So, Myomo has solid liquidity in the short-term. The company is without major long-term debts.

As noted earlier, Myomo was cash flow positive in Q4 2024, implying an extended cash runway. However, investors should be aware that any adverse changes (CMS reimbursement, expenses, revenues) could result in cash burn and reduce its cash runway.

MyoPro’s Niche Appeal and Competitive Vulnerabilities

Myomo’s 86% year-over-year growth in 2024 was driven by Medicare Part B beneficiaries. So, CMS reclassification was a watershed moment for Myomo. Although this guarantees reimbursement, MyoPro remains, in my view, a niche product. Its target market is uncertain. MyoPro candidates “must meet a comprehensive set of requirements” like long-term paralysis, detectable muscle signals, cognitive abilities, and strength conservation to effectively use the brace. There are prerequisites as well, including rehabilitation therapy, and as Myomo notes, "after a stroke or a traumatic injury, a large portion of survivors regain much or all of their function." Moreover, simpler and cheaper non-powered braces may be all that is needed for some people to become functional again.

Myomo also competes with exoskeleton suits and Neurolutions’ IpsiHand. The latter is a brain-computer interface (BCI) therapy device that uses brain signals to restore arm and hand movements. I point this all out because it can be easy to misinterpret the actual market opportunity. It is considerably smaller and more nuanced than “number of strokes per year.”

Critically, key foundational patents that Myomo licensed from MIT expired in 2023. The company does have a portfolio of 30+ patents covering its multi-joint Motion G product (which generates the vast majority of revenue), but the expiration of the MIT patent could lower barriers to entry for competitors. And as Myomo notes in its annual report, the publishing of fees by CMS “is expected to attract competition in the U.S.”

Obviously, no one is going to recreate the exact MyoPro and market it as their own. However, developing a similar device could be feasibly achieved. In the hands of a larger medical device company, it’s challenging to see how Myomo would compete. This has not happened yet (the actual market had been so small prior to reimbursement tailwinds), but one has to wonder how wide Myomo’s competitive moat is, especially considering recent advancements in robotics and AI.

Valuation Assumptions

Thanks to a declining stock price and some dilution, Myomo’s valuation has come down quite a bit since my last evaluation (from a $200 million EV to a $133 million EV), meriting another evaluation.

Earlier this year, I estimated that Myomo appeared priced for 25% growth and 10% margins (free cash flow). However, back then, the 2025 revenue estimate was $42.2 million. Due to Myomo's impressive revenue growth in 2024, this, too, has changed.

My main interest in performing a discounted cash flow analysis (DCF) is to get an idea about what expectations are implied by its valuation. We can then determine if the assumptions are reasonable.

So, the "valuation assessment" that follows was purposely tailored to get a result close to the current stock price. In a sense, I am conducting a "reverse DCF."

So, Myomo appears to be priced for 15% revenue growth and 15% FCF margins. This is more reasonable than previous assumptions. Due to the nature of the model, both figures represent "averages" over the projection period. In reality, growth and margins will take non-linear forms.

Next, my sensitivity analysis reveals how slight adjustments to key inputs affect valuation.

As expected, small changes to assumptions cause dramatic shifts in valuation.

Lastly, I performed a Monte Carlo simulation. This runs 10,000 iterations of the DCF model and accounts for variability in key inputs like terminal growth, margins, and beta. It provides a more dynamic and realistic view of share price outcomes.

Notably, financial models are only as good as their inputs (garbage in = garbage out) and the results are biased by my original inputs.

Based on expectations that are implied by Myomo’s valuation (as uncovered by my models), Myomo appears reasonably priced at this moment with considerable upside potential in the event it outperforms expectations.

Conclusion

In summation, while CMS reclassification has propelled Myomo into a viable business, existential threats (e.g., technological advancements, competition, the need for ongoing CMS reimbursement) remain palpable. Myomo spun out from MIT two decades ago based on myoelectric technology. A lack of profitability limited R&D spending. While its device has evolved (refined software, lightweight custom-fabricated design, multi-joints), the fundamental technology from two decades ago remains largely unchanged. I think it is more likely than not that Myomo’s technology will become inferior to newer technology (advanced robotics, AI-driven control, BCI). This is just my opinion.

My models reveal that if you’re long MYO, the bet is that Myomo performs slightly better than expected, and this is certainly possible in the short-term. But it is not because the market is wildly wrong in its assumptions. In fact, the market’s implied expectations still appear optimistic. So, again, it boils down to execution, and having a great product isn’t always enough.

In short, I am not convinced that Myomo has “staying power” and much of its valuation (as with any valuation) relies on very uncertain terminal growth assumptions.

MYO remains a cautious “Hold.” While I can see MYO's utility in a barbell portfolio, I remain concerned about its long-term prospects.

Risks

MYO is a microcap stock, so there are additional risks to consider. For starters, analyst coverage on MYO is limited. This contributes to information asymmetry. As evidenced by a stock beta of 2.37 (per Seeking Alpha), its stock is volatile. As a result, during times of market underperformance, MYO could drop dramatically and suddenly. Finally, Myomo still relies on equity offerings (e.g., December 2024), which causes dilution and diminishes shareholder returns.
